subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Official components from the provided rulebook text: 1 game board inside the box (used as scoring track), 84 image cards, 36 voting tokens in 6 different colors numbered 1 through 6, and 6 wooden rabbits (meeples/rabbeeples).

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Place the scoring board where all players can see and reach it. Shuffle all 84 image cards and deal starting hands. Place the remaining cards face down as a draw pile. No central tableau is built during setup; the display row is created each turn by the storyteller when submitted cards are revealed and numbered from left to right.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player chooses one wooden rabbit and places it on score 0. Each player receives 6 image cards as a hidden hand (do not reveal your hand to others). Voting tokens are distributed by player count: with 4 players, each player takes tokens 1-4; with 5 players, tokens 1-5; with 6 players, tokens 1-6.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: First storyteller is the first player to describe one of their cards aloud. That player chooses one card from their hand and gives a clue (word, phrase, sound, or cultural reference) without showing the card. All non-storytellers then choose one card from hand that best matches the clue and submit it face down to the storyteller.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Resolve each turn in this order: 1) Storyteller chooses one hand card and gives a clue aloud (card stays hidden). 2) Every other player chooses one hand card matching that clue and passes it face down to storyteller. 3) Storyteller shuffles all submitted cards together with their own card, then reveals them face up in random order. Number revealed cards left to right (1, 2, 3, etc.). 4) Non-storytellers secretly vote for the card they think is the storyteller’s card using their numbered voting token; storyteller does not vote. 5) Reveal votes and place each token on the voted card. 6) Score points and move rabbits on track. 7) Each player draws back up to 6 cards. 8) Storyteller role passes to the player on the left (clockwise).

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Storyteller action: choose exactly one card from hand and give one clue before reveal. Clue flexibility is broad: one word, multiple words, a sound, an invented phrase, or reference to an existing work (poetry line, song lyric, movie title, proverb, etc.). 

Non-storyteller submission action: choose exactly one card from hand that best fits clue and submit it hidden to storyteller. This is your bluff card for the round. 

Voting action: once cards are revealed and numbered, each non-storyteller chooses exactly one voting token matching the target card number and places it face down. Then all reveal simultaneously. Restriction: you may not vote for your own submitted card. 

Hand maintenance action: after scoring, all players draw one card to return hand size to 6. 

Role rotation: storyteller seat rotates clockwise every turn without exception.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Core clue-balance mechanic: the storyteller wants some—but not all—players to identify their card. If everyone or no one finds the storyteller card, storyteller scores 0. This creates a deliberate middle-target clue challenge. 

Bluff incentive mechanic: non-storytellers can score from votes placed on their submitted decoy cards (+1 per vote), so they are incentivized to submit plausible alternatives rather than obvious mismatches. 

Player-count voting-token scaling: each player only receives token numbers relevant to cards that will be revealed that turn (4-player uses 1-4, 5-player uses 1-5, 6-player uses 1-6). 

Variants listed in source text: 
- 3-player variant: each player has 7 cards instead of 6; each non-storyteller submits 2 cards, creating 5 revealed cards total. Special scoring tweak: if only one player finds storyteller card, that player and storyteller score 4 instead of 3. 
- Mimes or songs variant: storyteller may sing or mime clue relation; all other rules unchanged.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Scoring each turn: 
1) If all non-storytellers found storyteller card, OR none found it: storyteller scores 0; every other player scores 2. 
2) Otherwise (some but not all found storyteller card): storyteller scores 3; each player who correctly voted storyteller card also scores 3. 
3) Additionally, each non-storyteller scores +1 for every vote token placed on their submitted card. 

Move rabbits forward by points earned. Game end trigger: when the last card from the draw pile has been drawn. Winner: player farthest along scoring track. The provided rulebook excerpt does not define a formal tie-breaker; if tied at end in your group, use shared victory or house tie-break before starting play.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: 1) As storyteller in early rounds, aim for medium specificity: a clue that strongly matches your card but leaves room for one or two convincing decoys. 2) As non-storyteller, submit cards that could realistically attract votes from players who partially understand the clue. 3) Track players’ clue styles quickly—some players clue literally, others abstractly; adapt your votes and submissions to each style. 4) In first few turns, avoid burning all your broad-interpretation cards unless they secure major score swings; keep flexible cards for future storytelling turns.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Storyteller clue too precise: everyone finds your card and you score 0. 
2) Storyteller clue too abstract: nobody finds your card and you still score 0. 
3) Voting on the prettiest card instead of the clue-consistent card; this loses repeated points over a full game. 
4) Submitting obvious throwaway decoys that attract no votes, wasting the +1-per-vote scoring channel. 
5) Forgetting that you cannot vote for your own card and mentally anchoring on it anyway, which leads to rushed bad votes.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Clarity vs ambiguity as storyteller: maximizing direct recognition is not optimal; calibrated ambiguity is. 

Safe vote vs meta vote: do you pick the most clue-fitting image, or predict what this specific storyteller tends to choose? 

Point floor vs point ceiling as non-storyteller: submit a card likely to get at least one vote, or chase a high-variance decoy that might steal many votes but could also score zero.

metadata.notes: Primary source used: provided Dixit rulebook excerpt.
